under existing statutes, regulations, rules and court decisions, and assuming certain representations and compliance with certain

tax purposes and is not an item of tax preference for purposes of the federal alternative minimum taxes.  In the further opinion of Bond 
Counsel, interest on the Series 2021A Bonds is exempt from California personal income taxes.  See “TAX MATTERS” herein.

This cover page contains certain information for general reference only.  It is not intended to be a summary of the security for or the terms of 
the Series 2021A Bonds.  Investors are advised to read the entire Official Statement to obtain information essential to the making of an informed 
investment decision.  Capitalized terms used on this cover page not otherwise defined shall have the meanings set forth herein. 
The City of Vallejo, California (the “City”), is issuing its Water Revenue Bonds, Series 2021A (the “Series 2021A Bonds”), to provide funds to finance 
capital facilities needed to operate and improve the City’s water system, as more fully described herein.  See “ESTIMATED SOURCES AND USES OF 
FUNDS.”  Interest on the Series 2021A Bonds is payable on May 1 and November 1 of each year, commencing on May 1, 2021.  Principal of the Series 
2021A Bonds is payable on the dates set forth on the inside cover hereof.  The Series 2021A Bonds are being issued in fully registered form and, when 
issued, will be registered in the name of Cede & Co., as nominee of The Depository Trust Company (“DTC”), New York, New York.  DTC will act as 
securities depository of the Series 2021A Bonds.  Individual purchases of interests in the Series 2021A Bonds will be made in book-entry form only, in the 
principal amount of $5,000 or any integral multiple thereof.  Purchasers of such interests will not receive certificates representing their interests in the 
Series 2021A Bonds.  Principal of and interest on the Series 2021A Bonds are payable directly by Wells Fargo Bank, National Association, San Francisco, 
California, as trustee (the “Trustee”), to DTC, which is obligated in turn to remit such principal and interest to its DTC Participants for subsequent 
disbursement to the Beneficial Owners of the Series 2021A Bonds, as described herein.  See “APPENDIX F – DTC AND THE BOOK-ENTRY SYSTEM.”
The Series 2021A Bonds are subject to redemption prior to their respective maturities as described herein.  See “THE SERIES 2021A 
BONDS – Redemption of the Series 2021A Bonds.”
The Series 2021A Bonds are payable solely from a first lien on and pledge of the Net Revenues derived by the City from the operation of the City’s 
water system.  The principal of and interest on the Series 2021A Bonds shall not constitute a debt of the City, or a legal or equitable pledge, charge, lien 
or encumbrance upon any property of the City or upon any of its income, receipts or revenues except the Net Revenues and other funds pledged to the 
payment thereof as provided in the Indenture.  The City previously issued its Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2020A (the “Series 2020A Bonds”) 
in the original principal amount of $16,750,000, and its Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2020B (Federally Taxable) (the “Series 2020B Bonds”) 
in the original principal amount of $20,075,000, pursuant to the Indenture (as defined herein).  Additionally, the City currently has one outstanding 
State of California Safe Drinking Water State Revolving Fund loan received by the City from the State of California Department of Water Resources and 
outstanding, as of January 1, 2021, in the amount of $13,615 (the “2004 SDWSRF Loan”).  The Series 2021A Bonds are secured by a pledge of Net Revenues 
on a parity with the Series 2020A Bonds, the Series 2020B Bonds, and the 2004 SDWSRF Loan.  The Series 2021A Bonds will not be secured by amounts 
on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund established under the Indenture, or by any other reserve fund, and amounts on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund 
are not pledged to the payment of the Series 2021A Bonds.  See “SECURITY AND SOURCES OF PAYMENT FOR THE SERIES 2021A BONDS.”
THE SERIES 2021A BONDS ARE SPECIAL, LIMITED OBLIGATIONS OF THE CITY.  THE SERIES 2021A BONDS ARE NOT DEEMED TO 
CONSTITUTE A DEBT OR LIABILITY OF THE CITY, THE STATE OR OF ANY POLITICAL SUBDIVISION THEREOF WITHIN THE MEANING OF ANY 
CONSTITUTIONAL OR STATUTORY PROVISION, OR A PLEDGE OF THE FAITH AND CREDIT OF THE CITY, THE STATE OR OF ANY POLITICAL 
SUBDIVISION THEREOF, BUT ARE PAYABLE, EXCEPT TO THE EXTENT OF CERTAIN AMOUNTS HELD UNDER THE INDENTURE PLEDGED 
THEREFORE, SOLELY FROM NET REVENUES.  NEITHER THE FAITH AND CREDIT NOR THE TAXING POWER OF THE CITY, THE STATE OR 
OF ANY POLITICAL SUBDIVISION THEREOF IS PLEDGED TO THE PAYMENT OF THE PRINCIPAL OF OR THE INTEREST ON THE SERIES 2021A 
BONDS.  THE ISSUANCE OF THE SERIES 2021A BONDS DOES NOT DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY OR CONTINGENTLY OBLIGATE THE CITY, THE 
STATE OR ANY POLITICAL SUBDIVISION THEREOF TO LEVY OR TO PLEDGE ANY FORM OF TAXATION WHATSOEVER THEREFORE OR TO 
MAKE ANY APPROPRIATION FOR THEIR PAYMENT.
The scheduled payment of principal of and interest on the Series 2021A Bonds maturing on May 1 of the years 2028 through 2041, inclusive, 
May 1, 2046 and May 1, 2051, with CUSIP #s 919259BZ3, 919259CA7, 919259CB5, 919259CC3, 919259CD1, 919259CE9, 919259CF6, 919259CG4, 919259CH2, 
919259CJ8, 919259CK5, 919259CL3, 919259CM1, 919259CN9, 919259CP4, and 919259CQ2 (collectively, the “Insured Series 2021A Bonds”), when due will 
be guaranteed under a municipal bond insurance policy to be issued concurrently with the delivery of the Insured Bonds by BUILD AMERICA MUTUAL 
ASSURANCE COMPANY.

OFFICIAL STATEMENT 
$42,600,000 
City of Vallejo 
Water Revenue Bonds 
Series 2021A 
INTRODUCTION 
This Introduction is qualified in its entirety by reference to the more detailed information included and 
referred to elsewhere in this Official Statement.  The offering of the Series 2021A  Bonds to potential investors is 
made only by means of the entire Official Statement.  Terms used herein and not otherwise defined shall have the 
respective meanings assigned to t hem in “APPENDIX C – SUMMARY OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF THE 
INDENTURE – Definitions.” 
Purpose 
The purpose of this Official Statement, which includes the cover page and appendices hereto, is to set forth 
certain information concerning the offering by the City  of Vallejo, California (the “City”) of $42,600,000 aggregate 
principal amount of its Water Revenue Bonds, Series 2021A (the “Series 2021A Bonds”).   
The City is issuing its Series 2021A  Bonds to provide funds, together with other available moneys, to 
provide funds to finance capital facilities needed to operate and improve the City’s water system, See 
“ESTIMATED SOURCES AND USES OF FUNDS.” 
Authority for Issuance 
The Series 2021A  Bonds are being issued pursuant to an ordinance adopted by the City Council on  
March 10, 1992 (the “Ordinance”), pursuant to its authority under the City Charter and the constitution and laws of 
the State of California (the “State”) and a Water Revenue Bond Indenture, dated as of July  1, 2006 (the “Master 
Indenture”), by and between  the City and Wells Fargo Bank, National Association, as trustee (the “Trustee”), as 
amended and supplemented by the First Supplemental Water Revenue Bond Indenture, dated as of July 1, 2006 (the 
“First Supplemental Indenture”), the Second Supplemental Water Revenue Bond Indenture, dated as of November 1, 
2013 (the “Second Supplemental Indenture”), the Third Supplemental Water Revenue Bond Indenture, dated as of 
March 1, 2020 (the “Third Supplemental Indenture”), and the Fourth Supplemental Water Revenue Bond Indenture,  
dated as of February 1, 2021 (the “Fourth Supplemental Indenture, and, together with the Master Indenture, the First 
Supplemental Indenture , the Second Supplemental Indenture,  and the Third Supplemental Indenture, the 
“Indenture”). 
The City 
The City is a waterfront city located at the juncture of the Napa River with San Pablo Bay and Suisun Bay, 
in Solano County, California.  Neighboring communities include Benicia, American Canyon, Fairfield and Napa.  
The City is centrally located among nor thern California commerce centers, being 32 miles northeast of San 
Francisco, 22 miles north of Oakland, 57 miles southwest of Sacramento and 67 miles north of San Jose.  The City  
has a population of approximately 120,000. 
The City is a chartered city gove rned by a Mayor and six City Council members who are elected to 
four-year alternating terms.  The City adopted the Council -Manager form of operation in 1946.  See also 
“APPENDIX A – CERTAIN INFORMATION REGARDING THE CITY OF VALLEJO.” 
The City filed a petit ion under Chapter 9 in 2008 in the United States Bankruptcy Court for the Eastern 
District of California, Sacramento Division.  As part of its bankruptcy plan, the City restructured various general  
2 
fund obligations and renegotiated collective bargaining agreements with employees and reduced services.  The City 
emerged from bankruptcy in 2011 and has timely paid all of its obligations restructured by its bankruptcy plan.   
The Water Enterprise was not impaired or restricted in the 2008 bankruptcy proceeding.   The City has 
complied with its covenants under the Indenture and has made timely payments of principal of and interest on the 
2004 SDWSRF Loan (defined below under “–Parity Debt”), the Series 2020A Bonds (defined below under “–Parity 
Debt”) and Series 20 20B Bonds  (defined below under “ –Parity Debt”), and (prior to being paid off ) the 2000 
SDWSRF Loan (defined below under “ –Parity Debt”), the Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2006, and the 
Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2013.   
The Water Enterprise 
The Water Enterprise (defined below) is owned and operated by the City and managed by the Water 
Department of the City.  The Water Enterprise consists of approximately 605 miles of transmission and distribution 
mains, three water treatment plants and 26 pump stations.  See “THE WATER ENTERPRISE.” 
The financial operations of the Water Enterprise are accounted for in an enterprise fund held by the City 
known as the “Water Fund.”  All gross revenues of the Water Enterprise are deposited in the Water Fund.   
Security and Sources of Payment for the Series 2021A Bonds 
The payment of the principal of and interest on the Series 2021A Bonds is secured equally and ratably by a 
first lien on and pledge of the Net Revenues derived from the operation of the Water Ent erprise and the moneys and 
securities held by the Trustee in the funds under the Indenture, other than in the Parity Reserve Fund.  The pledge of 
Net Revenues to the Series 2021A  Bonds is on a parity with the pledge of Net Revenues for the Series 2020A 
Bonds, the Series 2020B Bonds, the 2004 SDWSRF Loan and any Additional Bonds or Parity Debt to be issued in 
the future.  The Series 2021A  Bonds and any other series of bonds issued under the Indenture are sometimes 
collectively referred to herein as the “Bond s.”  The Series 2021A Bonds will not be secured by amounts on deposit 
in the Parity Reserve Fund established under the Indenture or by any other reserve fund.  See “SECURITY AND 
SOURCES OF PAYMENT FOR THE SERIES 2021A BONDS – Additional Bonds and Parity Debt.” 
The Series 2021A  Bonds are special, limited obligations of the City.  The Series 2021A  Bonds are not 
deemed to constitute a debt or liability of the City, the State or of any political subdivision thereof within the 
meaning of any constitutional or st atutory provision, or a pledge of the faith and credit of the City, the State or of 
any political subdivision thereof, but are payable, except to the extent of certain amounts held under the Indenture 
pledged therefore, solely from Net Revenues.  Neither the faith and credit nor the taxing power of the City, the State 
or of any political subdivision thereof is pledged to the payment of the principal of or the interest on the Series 
2021A Bonds.  The issuance of the Series 2021A  Bonds does not directly or in directly or contingently obligate the 
City, the State or any political subdivision thereof to levy or to pledge any form of taxation whatsoever therefore or 
to make any appropriation for their payment. 
Parity Debt 
The City may at any time issue or incur Pa rity Debt payable from the Net Revenues and other funds as 
provided in the Indenture and secured by a lien and charge securing the Outstanding Bonds, subject to the conditions 
set forth in the Indenture.   
In 2000, the City received a State of California S afe Drinking Water State Revolving Fund loan (the “2000 
SDWSRF Loan”) in the amount of $6,675,000 from the State of California Department of Water Resources 
(“DWR”) pursuant to a Funding Agreement, dated July 24, 2000 (the “2000 Funding Agreement”), by and between 
the City and DWR.  Although the final maturity of the 2000 SDWSRF Loan is January 2, 2021, the 2000 SDWSRF 
Loan was fully paid off in June of 2020 and is no longer outstanding.   
In 2004, the City received a State of California Safe Drinking Water  State Revolving Fund loan (the “2004 
SDWSRF Loan”) in the amount of $68,080 from the DWR pursuant to a Funding Agreement, dated June 2, 2004  
3 
(the “2004 Funding Agreement”), by and between the City and DWR.  In addition, pursuant to the 2004 Funding 
Agreement, the DWR provided the City with a grant of $272,320 for the project.  The 2004 SDWSRF Loan and the 
grant were used to fund Water System s (as defined below in “THE ENTERPRISE – Water System s”) 
improvements.  As of January 1, 2021, the 2004 SDWSRF Loan was outstanding in the amount of $13,615.   
In February of 2020, the City issued its Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2020A, in the original 
principal amount of $16,750,000 (the “Series 2020A Bonds”), and Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2020B 
(Federally Taxable), in the original principal amount of $20,075,000 (the “Series 2020B Bonds”), pursuant to the 
Third Supplemental Indenture.  The Series 2020A Bonds were issued to refund all of the City’s Water Revenue 
Refunding Bonds, Series 2006, and to pay costs of issuance of the Series 2020A Bonds.  The Series 2020B Bonds, 
together with the Series 2020A Bonds, provided funds to refund and defease all of the City’s outstanding Water 
Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2013.  As of January 1, 2021,  the Series 2020A Bonds were outstanding in the 
amount of $13,180,000, and the Series 2020B Bonds were outstanding in the amount of $20,075,000. 
The Series 2021A Bonds will be issued on a parity with the 2004 SDWSRF Loan, the Series 2020A Bonds, 
and the Series 2020B Bonds .  See “SECURITY AND SOURCES OF PAYMENT FOR THE SERIES 2021A 
BONDS – Additional Bonds and Parity Debt.” 
Outstanding Debt

Rate Covenant 
The City covenants under the Indenture that, so long as any Bonds are outstanding, the City will fix, 
prescribe, revise and collect rates, fees and charges for the services and facilities furnished by the Water Enterprise 
which are sufficient to yield Net Revenues in each Fiscal Year so that the ratio of Net Revenues to annual Debt 
Service at the end of each Fiscal Year is not  less than 1.25:1.0.  See “SECURITY AND SOURCES OF PAYMENT 
FOR THE SERIES 2021A  BONDS – Rate Covenant.”  However, the City’s ability to increase such rates, fees and 
charges is subject to the limitations imposed by Proposition 218.  See “CONSTITUTIONAL LIM ITATIONS ON 
TAXES AND WATER RATES AND CHARGES – Articles XIIIC and XIIID of the California Constitution.” 
Continuing Disclosure 
The City has covenanted for the benefit of the owners and beneficial owners of the Series 2021A Bonds to 
provide certain financi al information and operating data relating to the City and its Water Enterprise by not later 
than 270 days following the end of the City’s Fiscal Year ( which presently ends on June 30), commencing 
March 31, 2022 , with the report for Fiscal Year 20 20-21, an d to provide notices of the occurrence of certain 
enumerated events.  See “CONTINUING DISCLOSURE” herein and “APPENDIX E – FORM OF CONTINUING 
DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT.” 
Other Matters 
This Official Statement speaks only as of its date, and the information and expressions of opinion contained 
herein are subject to change without notice, and neither delivery of this Official Statement nor any sale made 
hereunder shall, under any circumstances, create any implication that there has been no change in the affairs of the 
City since the date hereof.  This Official Statement, including any supplement or amendment hereto, is intended to 
be deposited with one or more repositories.

All references to and summaries of provisions of the Indenture are qualified in their entirety by reference to 
the full Indenture, copies of which are available for inspection at the offices of the City in Vallejo, California or at 
the corporate trust office of the Trustee in San Francisco, California. 
FINANCE PLAN 
The City expects to apply a portion of the proceeds of the Series 2021A Bonds and certain other City funds 
to finance the design and construction of certain capital improvements to the City’s  Water System (the “Project”).  
The Project consists of: various capital improvements included as a part of the City’s capital improvement plan.  See 
“WATER ENTERPRISE – CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM.” The total cost of the Project is expected to 
be approximately $64.5 million, of which approximately $51,272,855.76  is expected to be financed from procee ds 
of the Series 2021A Bonds and the balance is expected to be funded from City reserves.

THE SERIES 2021A BONDS 
General Description 
The Series 2021A Bonds are being issued in fully registered form and, when issued, will be registered in 
the name of Cede & Co., as nominee of The Depository Trust Company (“DTC”).  DTC will act as securities 
depository of the Series 2021A  Bonds.  Individual purchases of ownership interests in the Series 2021A  Bonds will 
be made in book-entry form only in denominations of $5,000 or any integral multiple thereof.  See “APPENDIX F – 
DTC AND THE BOOK-ENTRY SYSTEM.” 
The Series 2021A Bonds will be dated their date of initial delivery, and will bear interest at the rates and 
mature in the amounts and on the dates set forth on the inside cover page of this Official Statement.  Interest on the 
Series 2021A Bonds is payable on May 1 and November  1 of each year, commencing May 1, 2021 , calculated on 
the basis of a 360 -day year comprised of twelve 30 -day months.  Principal of and interest on the Series 2021A 
Bonds are payable by the Trustee to DTC, which is obligated in turn to  remit such principal and interest to DTC 
Participants for subsequent disbursement to the Beneficial Owners of the Series 2021A Bonds. 
Redemption of the Series 2021A Bonds 
Optional Redemption of the Series 2021A Bonds.  The Series 2021A Bonds maturing on o r after May 1, 
2032, are subject to optional redemption prior to maturity on any Business Day on or after May 1, 2031, in whole or 
in part, in a manner determined by the City and specified to the Trustee in writing by the City at Redemption Prices 
equal to the principal amount thereof to be redeemed together with accrued interest to the redemption date.  
5

subject to mandatory sinking fund redemption by lot on May 1 commencing on May 1, 2042 in the following 
respective years and in the following respective principal amounts by the application of Mandatory Sinking Fund 
Payments in the amounts stated below:

Upon any redemption of Series 2021A Bonds pursuant to the Indenture, the City may direct that an amount 
equal to the aggregate principal amount of Series 2021A  Bonds so redeemed shall be credited towards a part or all 
of any one or more yearly Mandatory Sinking Fund Payments required for the Series 2021A  Bonds.  Any such 
direction shall be in writing, shall be delivered to the Trustee for such series of Series 2021A  Bonds at least 60 days 
before the scheduled date of such Mandatory S inking Fund Payments are to be paid and shall state the years in 
which and the amounts by which such Mandatory Sinking Fund Payments are to be reduced. 
Selection of Series 2021A Bonds to be Redeemed 
Series 2021A Bonds shall be redeemed only in Authorized Denominations and in such amounts such that 
any remaining Series 2021A  Bonds not redeemed are in Authorized Denominations.  If less than all Series 2021A  
Bonds are to be redeemed and paid prior to maturity, such Series 2021A  Bonds shall be selected by the T rustee by 
such method as the Trustee shall deem fair and appropriate and as the Trustee may be directed by the City. 
In the case of a partial redemption of Series 2021A Bonds when Series 2021A Bonds of denominations 
greater than the minimum Authorized Denomination are then Outstanding, then for all purposes in connection with 
such redemption each principal amount equal to the minimum Authorized Denomination shall be treated as though it 
was a separate Series 2021A Bond of the minimum Authorized Denomination.  If it is determined that a portion, but 
not all, of the principal amount represented by any Bond is to be selected for redemption, then upon notice of 
intention to redeem such portion, the Owner of such Series 2021A  Bond or such Owner’s attorney or lega l 
representative shall forthwith present and surrender such Series 2021A Bond to the Trustee (1) for payment of the 
Redemption Price of the principal amount called for redemption, and (2) for exchange, without charge to the Owner 
thereof for a new Series 2 021A Bond or Series 2021A Bonds of the aggregate principal amount of the unredeemed 
portion of the principal amount of such Bond.  If the Owner of any such Series 2021A  Bond shall fail to present 
such Series 2021A  Bond to the Trustee for payment and exchan ge as aforesaid, said Bond shall, nevertheless, 
become due and payable on the redemption date to the extent of the principal amount called for redemption (and to 
that extent only).  
6 
The Trustee shall call Series 2021A Bonds for redemption and payment as herein provided upon receipt by 
the Trustee at least 30 days prior to the redemption date of a Written Request of the City.  Such request shall specify 
the principal amount of Series 2021A  Bonds and their maturities so to be called for redemption, the applica ble 
Redemption Price or prices and the provision or provisions above referred to pursuant to which such Series 2021A 
Bonds are to be called for redemption.   
Notice of Redemption  
Official notice of any such redemption shall be given by the Trustee on beha lf of the City by mailing a 
copy of an official redemption notice by first class mail at least 30 days and not more than 60 days prior to the 
redemption date to each Owner of the Series 2021A  Bonds to be redeemed at the address shown on the Bond 
Register or at such other address as is furnished in writing by such Owner to the Trustee. 
All official notices of redemption  shall be dated and shall state the Series of Series 2021A  Bonds to be 
redeemed, the redemption date, the Redemption Price;  if less than all Outstanding Series 2021A Bonds are to be 
redeemed, the identification number and the respective principal amounts to be redeemed of the Series 2021A Bonds 
to be redeemed;  that on the redemption date the Redemption Price will become due and payable upon eac h such 
Series 2021A Bond or portion thereof called for redemption, and that interest thereon shall cease to accrue from and 
after said date; and the place where such Series 2021A Bonds are to be surrendered for payment of the Redemption 
Price, which place of payment shall be the Corporate Trust Office of the Trustee for the payment of the Series 
2021A Bonds. 
Any notice of redemption of any Series 2021A Bonds subject to redemption pursuant to the Indenture may 
specify that the redemption is contingent upon the deposit of moneys with the Trustee in an amount sufficient to pay 
the Redemption Price of all Series 2021A Bonds or portions of Series 2021A Bonds that are to be redeemed on that 
date. 
Official notice of redemption having been given, the Series 2021A  Bonds or portions of Series 2021A 
Bonds so to be redeemed shall, on the redemption date, become due and payable at the Redemption Price therein 
specified, and from and after such date (unless the City shall default in the payment of the Redemption Price) suc h 
Series 2021A Bonds or portions of the Series 2021A Bonds shall cease to bear interest.  Upon surrender of such 
Series 2021A Bonds for redemption in accordance with said notice, such Series 2021A Bonds shall be paid by the 
Trustee at the Redemption Price.   Installments of interest due on or prior to the redemption date shall be payable as 
herein provided for payment of interest.  Upon surrender for any partial redemption of any Bond, there shall be 
prepared for the Owner a new Bond or Series 2021A  Bonds of  the same maturity in the amount of the unpaid 
principal.  All Series 2021A Bonds that have been redeemed shall be canceled and destroyed by the Trustee in 
accordance with the Indenture and shall not be reissued.

SECURITY AND SOURCES OF PAYMENT FOR THE SERIES 2021A BONDS 
Net Revenue Pledge 
The Series 2021A  Bonds are revenue obligations of the City and are payable as to both principal and 
interest exclusively from Net Revenues and from the other funds pledged under the Indenture.  All Net Revenues are 
pledged to secure the payment of the principal of, redemption premium, if any, and interest on the Bonds and any  
7 
Parity Debt in accordance with their terms, subject only to the provisions of the Indenture permitting the application 
thereof for the purposes and on the terms and condit ions set forth in the Indenture.  The pledge of Net Revenues to 
the Series 2021A Bonds is on a parity with the pledge of Net Revenues for the 2004 SDWSRF Loan , the Series 
2020A Bonds, the Series 2020B Bonds, and any additional Bonds or Parity Debt to be is sued in the future.  See “– 
Additional Bonds and Parity Debt.”  The Net Revenues are irrevocably pledged to the punctual payment of the 
principal of, redemption premium, if any, and interest on the Bonds, including the Series 2021A Bonds. 
“Net Revenues ” is defined under the Indenture to mean, with respect to any period, the amount of the 
Gross Revenues received during such period less the amount of Operating Expenses becoming payable during such 
period. 
“Gross Revenues” is defined under the Indenture to mea n all gross income and revenue received by the 
City from the ownership and operation of the Water Enterprise, including (a)  all fees and charges received by the 
City for the services of the Water Enterprise, (b) all other income and revenue however derived by the City from the 
ownership and operation of the Water Enterprise or arising from the Water Enterprise, and (c)  all sums deposited, or 
required under the Indenture to be deposited, in the Water Fund; but excluding (x)  the proceeds of any ad valorem 
property taxes received by the City to pay debt service on any outstanding obligations of the City, and (y)  any 
contributed capital (other than connection fees).  “Gross Revenues” does not include Contract Modification 
Revenues received from the United States pursuant to Contract P04626-87-00002 and Modification No. P00027. 
“Operating Expenses ” is defined under the Indenture, as amended under the Third Supplemental 
Indenture, to mean the reasonable and necessary costs of maintaining and operating the Water Ent erprise, calculated 
on the basis of generally accepted accounting principles, including (among other things) the reasonable expenses of 
management and repair and other expenses necessary to maintain and preserve the Water Enterprise in good repair 
and work ing order, and reasonable amounts for administration that are charged directly or apportioned to the 
operation of the Water Enterprise, such as salaries , wages and benefits of employees, overhead, insurance, taxes (if 
any) and other similar costs, but excl uding (a)  depreciation, replacement and obsolescence charges or reserves 
therefore or other bookkeeping entries of a similar nature, and (b)  interest on the Bonds and any Parity Debt.  See 
“AMENDMENTS TO INDENTURE.” 
“Enterprise” or “Water Enterprise” is defined in the Indenture to mean any and all facilities of the City 
for the production, storage, treatment and distribution of water for domestic use, industrial use, fire protection, 
recreation or any other public or private use and all other appurtenances necessary, useful or convenient for the 
production, storage, treatment and distribution of water and any necessary lands, rights of way and other real or 
personal property useful in connection therewith. 
“Parity Debt” is defined in the Indenture as any  indebtedness, installment sale obligation, lease obligation 
or other obligation of the City for borrowed money having an equal lien and charge upon the Net Revenues, 
therefore payable on a parity with the Bonds (whether or not any Bonds are Outstanding). 
The Series 2021A  Bonds are special, limited obligations of the City.  The Series 2021A  Bonds are not 
deemed to constitute a debt or liability of the City, the State or of any political subdivision thereof within the 
meaning of any constitutional or statutory p rovision, or a pledge of the faith and credit of the City, the State or of 
any political subdivision thereof, but are payable, except to the extent of certain amounts held under the Indenture 
pledged therefore, solely from Net Revenues.  Neither the faith and credit nor the taxing power of the City, the State 
or of any political subdivision thereof is pledged to the payment of the principal of or the interest on the Series 
2021A Bonds.  The issuance of the Series 2021A  Bonds does not directly or indirectly or contingently obligate the 
City, the State or any political subdivision thereof to levy or to pledge any form of taxation whatsoever therefore or 
to make any appropriation for their payment. 
The Indenture provides that all funds and accounts created purs uant to the Indenture constitute trust funds 
in favor of the Owners of the Bonds, until so applied for the purpose set forth in the Indenture.  
8 
Flow of Funds 
The City has covenanted that all Gross Revenues, when and as received, will be deposited and held b y the 
City in the Water Fund and will be accounted for through and held in trust in the Water Fund, and the City has no 
beneficial right or interest in any of such moneys except only as provided in the Indenture.  The City has covenanted 
and agreed to main tain the Water Fund at all times so long as any Bonds are Outstanding under the Indenture.  All 
Gross Revenues and Net Revenues, whether held by the City or deposited with the Trustee, will nevertheless be 
disbursed, allocated, and applied solely to the uses and purposes described below. 
All amounts in the Water Fund required to pay Operating Expenses of the Water Enterprise shall be applied 
for such purpose from time to time by the City.  So long as any Bonds are Outstanding, the City shall transfer the 
remaining money in the Water Fund to the Trustee as required for deposit into the following respective funds (each 
of which the Trustee shall establish, maintain and hold in trust for the benefit of the Owners of the Bonds) in the 
following amounts, in the f ollowing order of priority, the requirements of each such fund (including the making up 
of any deficiencies in any such fund resulting from lack of Net Revenues sufficient to make any earlier required 
deposit) at the time of deposit to be satisfied before any deposit is made to any fund subsequent in priority; provided 
that on a parity with such deposits the Trustee may set aside or transfer amounts with respect to outstanding Parity 
Debt as provided in the proceedings for such Parity Debt (which shall be proportionate in the event such amounts 
are insufficient to provide for all deposits required as of any date to be made with respect to the Bonds and such 
Parity Debt): 
Interest Fund.  The City will transfer to the Trustee and the Trustee will set aside in the Interest 
Fund on or before the third Business Day prior to each interest payment date an amount equal to the interest 
becoming due and payable on the Outstanding Bonds (excluding interest for which there are moneys on 
deposit in the Interest Fund from the proceeds of any Series of Bonds or other source to pay such interest). 
Principal Fund; Sinking Accounts .  The City will transfer to the Trustee and the Trustee will set 
aside in the Principal Fund on or before the third Business Day prior to each principal or mandatory sinking 
fund payment date an amount equal to (a) the amount of Bond Obligation becoming due and payable on the 
Outstanding Serial Bonds, plus (b) the mandatory sinking fund payments to be paid into the respective 
Sinking Accounts for the Term Bonds; provided that if the City certifies to the Trustee that any principal 
payments are expected to be refunded on or prior to their respective due dates or paid from excess amounts 
on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund or other bond reserve fund upon such payment, no amounts need be 
set aside towards such principal to be so refunded or paid.  All of the aforesaid mandatory sinking fund 
payments will be made without priority of any payment into any one such Sinking Account over any other 
such payment. 
Parity Reserve Fund or other Bond Reserve Fund.  Pursuant to the Indenture, as amended pursuant 
to the Third Supplemental Indenture, upon the occurrence of any deficiency in the Parity Reserve Fund 
established pursuant to the Indenture,  or other bond res erve fund established pursuant to a Supplemental 
Indenture, the City will transfer to the Trustee and the Trustee will set aside in the Parity Reserve Fund or 
other bond reserve fund, as applicable, an amount equal to the aggregate amount of each unrepleni shed 
prior withdrawal from the Parity Reserve Fund or other bond reserve fund, as applicable, until there is on 
deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund an amount equal to the Reserve Fund Requirement  or in the other bond 
reserve fund an amount equal to the rese rve fund requirement for such other bond reserve fund as set forth 
in the Supplemental Indenture . Pursuant to the Fourth Supplemental Indenture, the Series 2021A 
Bonds will not be secured by amounts on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund or by any other res erve 
fund.  Currently there are no funds on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund. 
Any Net Revenues remaining in the Water Fund after the transfers described above, except as otherwise 
provided in a Supplemental Indenture, will be held free and clear of the I ndenture by the City and it may use and 
apply such Net Revenues for any lawful purpose of the City, including the redemption of Bonds upon the terms and 
conditions set forth in the Supplemental Indenture relating to such Bonds and the purchase of Bonds as and when 
and at such prices as it may determine.  
9 
If, two days prior to any principal payment date, interest payment date or mandatory sinking fund 
redemption date, the amounts on deposit in the Interest Fund and Principal Fund, including the Sinking Accounts 
therein, are insufficient to make such payments, the Trustee will immediately notify the City, by telephone, of such 
deficiency and direct that the City transfer the amount of such deficiency to the Trustee on such payment date.  The 
City has covenanted  and agreed to transfer to the Trustee from any Net Revenues in its possession the amount of 
such deficiency on the principal, interest or mandatory redemption date referenced in such notice. 
Parity Reserve Fund 
The Trustee has established and will maintai n and hold in trust for so long as Parity Debt to be secured 
thereby remain outstanding, a special fund designated as the “Parity Reserve Fund.”  Amounts on deposit in the 
Parity Reserve Fund are pledged to the payment of any Parity Debt to be secured by t he Parity Reserve Fund and 
will be applied only for such purposes as permitted in the Indenture.   
Pursuant to the Fourth Supplemental Indenture, the Series 2021A  Bonds will not be secured by 
amounts on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund or by any other re serve fund.  Currently there are no funds 
on deposit in the Parity Reserve Fund.   See “APPENDIX C –  SUMMARY OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF 
THE INDENTURE.” 
Rate Covenant 
The rates for use of the Water Enterprise are set by the City Council and are not subject to review by any 
state or local governmental agency.  The City has covenanted that it will fix, prescribe, revise and collect rates, fees 
and charges for the services and facilities furnished by the Water Enterprise during each Fiscal Year which (together 
with other funds accumulated from Gross Revenues and which are lawfully available to the City for payment of any 
of the following amounts during such Fiscal Year) are at least sufficient to pay all Operating Expenses estimated to 
become due and payable in suc h Fiscal Year, Debt Service on the Bonds and any Parity Debt, all other payments 
required by the Indenture or any Parity Debt instruments or all payments required to meet any obligations of the City 
which are charges, liens, encumbrances upon or payable from the Gross Revenues or Net Revenues.  In addition, the 
City has covenanted that it will fix, prescribe, revise and collect rates, fees and charges so as to yield Net Revenues 
during such Fiscal Year equal to at least 1.25 times the annual Debt Service co ming due and payable in such Fiscal 
Year on Outstanding Bonds and any Parity Debt.  The City may make adjustments from time to time in such fees 
and charges and may make such classification thereof as it deems necessary, but such fees and charges will not be 
reduced so as to be insufficient to provide adequate Gross Revenues for the purposes described above.  For 
information on the possible limitation on the City’s ability to comply with the rate covenant because of Proposition 
218, see “CONSTITUTIONAL LIMITATIONS ON TAXES AND WATER RATES AND CHARGES – Articles 
XIIIC and XIIID of the California Constitution.” 
Additional Bonds and Parity Debt 
The City may at any time issue additional Series of Bonds or Parity Debt payable from the Net Revenues 
and other funds and secured by a lien and charge securing the Outstanding Bonds on a parity with the Bonds, subject 
to the following conditions precedent: (a) no Event of Default shall have occurred and then be continuing; (b) the 
aggregate principal amount of Bonds issued under the Indenture will not exceed any limitation imposed by law or 
otherwise; (c) there shall be deposited in the Parity Reserve Fund an amount of money so as to increase the amount 
on deposit therein to the Reserve Fund Requirement, if applicable; an d (d) the Net Revenues, calculated on sound 
accounting principles, as shown by the books of the City for the latest Fiscal Year or any more recent 12 -month 
period selected by the City ending not more than 60 days prior to the adoption of the Supplemental I ndenture 
pursuant to which such Series of Bonds are issued or the instrument pursuant to which such Parity Debt is issued, as 
shown by the books of the City, plus, at the option of the City, any or all of the items designated in (i) and (ii) below, 
but excluding connection charges, shall at least equal 125% of the Maximum Annual Debt Service on all Bonds and 
Parity Debt to be Outstanding immediately subsequent to the issuance of such Series of Bonds or Parity Debt.  The 
items any or all of which may be adde d to such Net Revenues for the purpose of issuing or incurring additional 
Series of Bonds or Parity Debt under the Indenture are the following:  
10 
(i) An allowance for Net Revenues from any additions to or improvements or extensions, of the 
Water Enterprise to be made with the proceeds of such additional Series of Bonds or Parity Debt, 
and also for Net Revenues from any such additions, improvements or extensions which have been 
made from moneys from any source but in any case which, during all or any part of such Fiscal 
Year or such 12-month period, were not in service, all in an amount equal to 95% of the estimated 
additional average annual Net Revenues to be derived from such additions, improvements and 
extensions for the first 36 -month period in which each addit ion, improvement or extension is 
respectively to be in operation, all as shown by a certificate of the City filed with the Trustee. 
(ii) An allowance for earnings arising from any increase in the charges made for service from the 
Water Enterprise which has beco me effective prior to the incurring of such additional Series of 
Bonds or Parity Debt but which, during all or any part of such Fiscal Year or such 12- month 
period, was not in effect, in an amount equal to 95% of the amount by which the Net Revenues 
would have been increased if such increase in charges had been in effect during the whole of such 
Fiscal Year or such 12 -month period, all as shown by a certificate of the City filed with the 
Trustee. 
In the event additional assets or revenues are included withi n the definition of “Net Revenues” by a 
Supplemental Indenture, such additional assets or revenues shall be included in the calculations described above, as 
appropriate, as if such additional assets or revenues had always been included in Net Revenues. 
“Maximum Annual Debt Service ” is defined under the Indenture as the greatest amount of principal and 
interest coming due on the Bonds and Parity Debt in the Fiscal Year in which the calculation is made or any 
successive Fiscal Year using the principles and assumptions set forth under the definition of Debt Service. 
“Debt Service ” is defined under the Indenture to mean, during any period of computation, the amount 
obtained for such period by totaling the following amounts:  (a) the Bond Obligation of all Outstanding Serial Bonds 
and all Parity Debt coming due and payable by their terms in such period; (b)  the minimum Bond Obligation of all 
Outstanding Term Bonds and all Parity Debt scheduled to be redeemed by operation of mandatory sinking fund 
deposits in such period, together with any premium thereon; and (c)  the interest which would be due during such 
period on the aggregate principal amount of Bonds and Parity Debt which would be Outstanding in such period if 
the Bonds or Parity Debt are retired as scheduled,  but deducting and excluding from such aggregate amount the 
amount of Bonds or Parity Debt no longer Outstanding; provided that for purposes of determining compliance with 
the test for the issuance of additional Bonds or Parity Debt and the rate covenant a nd the amount of the Reserve 
Fund Requirement, certain provisions of the Indenture will be applied.  See “APPENDIX C –  SUMMARY OF 
CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF THE INDENTURE – Definitions.” 
The City has existing indebtedness that is on a parity with the Series 2021A Bonds.  In 2004, the City 
received the 2004 SDWSRF Loan in the amount of $68,080 from the DWR pursuant to the 2004 Funding 
Agreement.  The 2004 SDWSRF Loan was used to fund Water System improvements.  The interest rate on the 2004 
SDWSRF is 0.0%, and th e 2004 SDWSRF Loan matures on January 1, 2025.  Semiannual principal payments are 
due on January 1 and July 1.  As of January 1, 2021, the 2004 SDWSRF Loan was outstanding in the amount of 
$13,615. 
In February of 2020, the City issued its Series 2020A Bonds and Series 2020B Bonds, pursuant to the Third 
Supplemental Indenture.  The Series 2020A Bonds were issued in the original principal amount of $16,750,000, and 
as of January 1, 2021, were outstanding in the amount of $13,180,000 . The Series 2020B Bonds we re issued in the 
original principal amount of $20,075,000, and as of October 1, 2020, were outstanding in the amount of 
$20,075,000.   
The Series 2021A Bonds will be issued on a parity with the 2004 SDWSRF Loan, the Series 2020A Bonds, 
and the Series 2020B  Bonds.  Nothing in the Indenture prohibits or impairs the authority of the City to issue bonds 
or other obligations which are unsecured or which are secured by a lien on Net Revenues which is subordinate to the 
lien established under the Indenture, upon such terms and in such principal amount as the City may determine.   
11 
Refunding Bonds 
Notwithstanding any provisions in the Indenture, there is no limitation on the ability of the City to issue any 
Bonds at any time to refund any outstanding Bonds or Parity Debt; provided, however, that the Maximum Annual 
Debt Service with respect to any such refunding Bonds shall not exceed 1. 10 times the Maximum Annual Debt 
Service with respect to the Bonds or Parity Debt being refunded.

Rate Stabilization Fund 
The City covenants under the Indenture to maintain and hold a Rate Stabilization Fund. The City may 
deposit Net Revenues or any othe r lawfully available funds in the Rate Stabilization Fund as the City may 
determine, provided that deposits for each Fiscal Year may be made until (but not after) 120 days following the end 
of such Fiscal Year.  The City may withdraw amounts from the Rate Stabilization Fund for inclusion in Gross 
Revenues for any Fiscal Year, but only until (but not after) 120 days after the end of such Fiscal Year.  Withdrawals 
from the Rate Stabilization Fund may be made at any time by the City for any lawful purpose of t he Water 
Enterprise.  See “APPENDIX C – SUMMARY OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF THE INDENTURE.”   
Following the issuance of the City of Vallejo’s Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2006 (the “Series 
2006 Bonds”), the City deposited $4,000,000 into the Rate Sta bilization Fund.  In Fiscal Year 2015 -16, the City 
withdrew $1,500,000 from the Rate Stabilization Fund for inclusion in Gross Revenues for such Fiscal Year.  As of  
January 1, 2021, the balance of the Rate Stabilization Fund is $2,500,000. 
THE CITY 
The City is a waterfront city located at the juncture of the Napa River with San Pablo Bay and Suisun Bay, 
in Solano County, California.  Neighboring communities include Benicia, American Canyon, Fairfield and Napa.  
The City is centrally located among northern California commerce centers, being 32 miles northeast of San 
Francisco, 22 miles north of Oakland, 57 miles southwest of Sacramento and 67 miles north of San Jose.   The City 
has a population of approximately 120,000.   
Governmental Organization 
The City is a chartered city governed by a Mayor and six City Council members who are elected to 
four-year alternating terms.  The City adopted the Council -Manager form of operation in 1946.  The City Council 
appoints the City Manager who is responsible for the day-to-day administration of City business, the coordination of 
all departments of the City, including the Water Enterprise, and carrying out the policies established by the City 
Council. 
The Water Director exercises general supervision over the Water Enterprise.  For Fiscal Year 2019-20, the 
City employed 101 employees in the Water Department, and 17 employees, performing water billing and collection 
and meter reading services for the Water Enterprise, are assigned to the City’s Finance Department.  For the Fiscal 
Year 2019-20, the Water Enterprise had revenues of approximately $46.4 million and expenditures of approximately 
$36.1 million. 
THE WATER ENTERPRISE 
General 
The City is a public water supplier that purchases, treats, distributes and sells water through its Water 
Enterprise.  The Water Enterprise serves an area of approximately 28.6 square miles, a current population of more 
than 124,000 and approximately 38,000 connections.  The service area of the Water Enterprise includes all area 
within the City limits and the adjacent unincorporated western parts of Solano County.  The City also serves a small 
number of customers in unincorporated Napa County and provides treated water under contract to Travis Air Force 
Base in Fairfield, California. 
The Water Systems 
The Water Enterprise is made up of two components: (i) the City and Mare Island water system (the “City 
Water System”), and (ii) the Lakes water system (the “Lakes Water System” and, together with the City Water 
System, the “Water Systems”).  Each of the Water Systems has its own rate structure and capital improvement 
program.  The City also has a service contract with the Travis Air Force Base (the “Travis Water Service Contract”)  
14 
to provide wholesale finished drinking water to the Travis Air Force Base m eeting all federal and State drinking 
water standards.     
The City Water System serves users within the City limits and includes Mare Island.  It consists of the 
Fleming Hill Water Treatment Plant (the “Fleming Hill WTP”), the main water treatment facilit y for the City, and 
related storage and distribution facilities.  The treatment plant is designed to treat 42 million gallons of water per day 
(“mgd”) and the distribution system has storage capacity of 80 mgd.  The maximum daily water demand for the last 
two years has been approximately 19 mgd. 
The Lakes Water System serves users located in northern Solano County and southeastern Napa County.  It 
consists of the Green Valley Water Treatment Plant (the “Green Valley WTP”) and associated storage and 
distribution facilities.  The treatment plant is designed to treat 1.0 mgd.  The maximum daily water demand for the 
last two years has been approximately 0.7 mgd. 
The City provides raw water to the Travis Air Force Base (the “Air Force Base”) for treatment pursuan t to 
the Travis Water Service Contract.  Since the 1950’s, the City has sold treated water to the Air Force Base and 
operated the Travis Water Treatment Plant (the “Travis WTP”) pursuant to the Travis Water Service Contract.  The 
Air Force Base is responsi ble for the storage, distribution and monitoring of the water once it leaves the Travis 
WTP.  The maximum daily water demand for the last two years has been approximately 3 mgd.   
Overview of System Facilities 
The Water Enterprise utilizes seven pumping st ations to convey the raw water supply to the three water 
treatment plants and several large water customers.  T wo additional pumping stations deliver raw water directly to 
two golf courses.  After the treatment process,  potable water travels throughout the water distribution system via 26 
pump stations  for storage or to customers .  There are approximately 605 miles of transmission and distribution 
pipelines.    
Pump Stations .  All pump stations are electronically driven.  The City has a stationary back up d iesel 
generator at the Shadow Ridge Pump Station. The City is in the process of purchasing mobile, trailer-mounted diesel 
generators.  At this time, the City rents mobile generators when needed due to power outages.   
In fall 2019, Public Safety Power Shut downs by Pacific Gas & Electric Company (“PG&E”), as further 
described herein, interrupted power to certain pump stations in the City’s Lakes Water System and the Cordelia 
Complex.  See “RISK FACTORS – Public Safety Power Shutdowns.” 
Transmission Mains on the Raw Water Supply System .  A brief description of the operational history for 
each transmission main on the raw water supply system is listed below: 
Cache Line from Cache Slough to Cordelia Reservoir .  This pipeline runs through an area having a 
high gr oundwater table with high concentrations of salts, and does not have cathodic protection.  There 
have been minimal reported leaks.   A portion of this  pipeline is used as an alternate raw water supply line 
for the Travis WTP. 
North Bay Aqueduct Extension fr om North Bay Aqueduct Cordelia Forebay to Cordelia Reservoir .  
This 39-inch pipeline runs along Mangels Boulevard and Green Valley Road, crosses I -80 and into Lopes 
Road.  It ties into the Cache Main at its intersection with Lopes Road near Fermi Dr ive.  T his line has 
cathodic protection. 
Cache Line between Cordelia Pump Station and Borges Summit.  There have been reported leaks on 
this pipeline.  It does not have cathodic protection.  A section of this line passes through a timber tunnel 
near Borges Summit. 
Cache Line between Borges Summit and Borges Summit Reservoir .  There has been an absence of 
reported leaks on this pipeline.  It does not have cathodic protection.  
15 
Cache Line between Borges Summit and Fleming Hill.  There has been an absence of reported leaks 
on this pipeline.  It does not have cathodic protection. 
Monticello Line.  This pipeline runs from the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation  Terminal Reservoir to 
Cordelia Pump Station, including through an area having a high groundwater table with high concentrations 
of salts.  It does not have cathodic protection.  There has been an absence of reported leaks in this pipeline.  
This line has a normally closed tie-in to the Gordon Valley line. 
Jameson Canyon Line .  Th is mixed-diameter pipeline runs approximately  10 miles from Cordelia 
Reservoir to Fleming Hill WTP.  It has cathodic protection. 
Green Valley Line .  Though this pipeline extends to Fleming Hill from Diversion Dam, water has 
not been delivered to the Fleming Hill WTP through this line for several year s.  The water from Diversion 
Dam is totally consumed by residences in the Lakes Systems. 
Storage.  There are two storage reservoirs on the supply system for the City Water System.  The first is the 
Cordelia Reservoir which is an octagon-shaped, concrete-lined, open-surface reservoir with a capacity of 15 million 
gallons.  This reservoir serves as a junction for the commingling of water from the Lake Berryessa Reservoir and the 
Sacramento River (North Bay Aqueduct).   The second reservoir is Borges Summit Res ervoir, a 60 -million gallon 
open-top reservoir within the City limits, which was constructed in 1968.  This reservoir provides emergency supply 
against service interruptions in the raw water supply system.  In terms of present demands, the storage capacity  of 
this reservoir is equivalent to two maximum demand days or three average demand days. 
There are three lakes that store water for the Lakes Water System.  Lakes Frey and Madigan are located in 
northern Solano County.  The City owns both lakes and the su rrounding watershed land.  Water flows from Lake 
Madigan into Lake Frey and then into Diversion Dam , from which the water continues to flow through a pipe into 
the Green Valley WTP located at the end of Green Valley Road.  Lake Curry is the largest lake in  the Lakes Water 
System with a maximum storage capacity of 10,700 acre -feet (“AF”). However, Lake Curry is not currently used as 
a supply source due to a lack of conveyance facilities and is not included in the City’s 20 -year water supply 
projections.  See “– Water Supply – Lake Curry.” 
Water Treatment Plants.  The Water Enterprise maintains and operates three water treatment plants (each a 
“WTP”) with a combined total rated capacity of 50.5 mgd. 
Fleming Hill WTP .  The Fleming Hill WTP is the main water tre atment facility for the City and the only 
water treatment facility serving the City Water System.  The Fleming Hill WTP was originally built in 1953 and 
upgraded in 1968.  From 1993 to 1996, the City undertook a modernization and expansion project to bring the 
plant’s capacity to 42 mgd to meet projected increases in water demand, improve plant performance, and enable the 
City to meet the requirements of the Surface Water Treatment Rules (as further described herein) and other state and 
federal water qualit y regulations.  Treatment processes are coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation, filtration and 
disinfection with primary disinfection by ozonation. 
Green Valley WTP.  The Green Valley WTP was constructed in 1998 and serves the Lakes Water System.  
It has a capacity of 1.0 mgd and treats lake water   and Solano Project water available through an exchange 
arrangement with the Solano Irrigation District. .  The treatment process originally consisted of coa gulation, 
flocculation, sedimentation, filtration and disinfection.  In 2006, magnetic -ion exchange pretreatment was added to 
reduce total organic carbon.  The Green Valley WTP was subsequently modified to meet U.S. Environmental 
Protection Agency (“EPA”) and State regulations.  See “WATER SYSTEM REGULATORY RE QUIREMENTS – 
Federal Requirements.”   
Travis WTP.  Travis WTP serves the Travis Air Force Base and is a complete, conventional 7.5 mgd plant.  
Treatment processes consist of coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation, filtration and disinfection with  chlorine as 
the primary disinfectant.  In 2008, the City completed the construction of the Travis – Beck Avenue Pump Station 
that allowed the City to pump Solano Project water from the Cordelia Reservoir into Travis WTP during the winter 
months of low North Bay Aqueduct water quality.  
16 
Water Supply 
The Water Systems are supplied solely from surface water.  No groundwater sources are used.  The City 
conveys surface water from various sources into its three water treatment plants for distribution to its customers.  
The surface water sources are described below.  Water purchases are considered “Operating Expenses” and, 
therefore, are payable prior to payment of debt service on Bonds. 
Vallejo Permit Water .  The City holds Appropriative Water Rights License No. 7848 with  the State of 
California Water Resources Control Board (the “SWRCB”), which was issued in August 1966 and provides what is 
commonly referred to as Vallejo Permit Water.  The license prescribes a maximum diversion of 31.52 cubic feet per 
second each year, w hich corresponds to an annual amount of 22,780 AF of water.  Like State Water Project water, 
Vallejo Permit Water is delivered from the North Bay Aqueduct pumping facility located at Ba rker Slough to the 
Cordelia Forebay and from there to Fleming Hill WTP.   
The City has rights to transport Vallejo Permit Water  through North Bay Aqueduct project facilities .  The 
conveyance of such Vallejo Permit Water is governed by Amendment  No. 10 to the Water Supply Contract , dated 
November 15, 1985, between DWR and the Solano County Water Agency (the “ SCWA”). Conveyance of Vallejo 
Permit Water is limited by contract to a maximum of 17,287 AF, or 78% of the water right, per year.  Since the 
limitation is not based on a physical capacity constraint of the North Bay Aqueduct, an additional 5,493 AF could be 
available upon execution of an amendment to the existing agreement between DWR and the SCWA.  
Although the City uses Vallejo Permit Water at a variable rate throughout the year, it is a more dependable 
supply than the Sta te Water Project water.   The City’s 1966 water right is considered a senior right.  From June 
2014 to November 2014 and May 2015 to November 2015, Vallejo Permit Water was curtailed due to drought 
conditions.  Nevertheless, from a water supply planning st andpoint, the City has assumed Vallejo Permit Water as a 
reliable water source during dry year conditions .  If Vallejo Permit Water is not used, it cannot be stored and 
therefore is lost to the City. 
Solano Project.  The Solano Project is a federal water p roject of the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation. The 
SCWA is the managing wholesaler for Solano County agencies for purchase of Solano Project water.  Solano  
Project water is stored in Lake Berryessa and is released down Putah Creek from Monticello Dam before bei ng re-
captured by Putah Diversion Dam approximately 13 miles downstream.  The water is diverted through the Putah 
South Canal and conveyed approximately 33 miles to the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation Terminal Reservoir in 
Cordelia where the City then pumps water to Fleming Hill WTP and seasonally to Travis WTP.   
The City is a Solano Project Participating Agency and has a contractual agreement with the SCWA.  The 
City has contract rights for up to 14,600 AF annually from the Solano Project.  In addition, if the  City does not use 
its entire annual allocation of Solano Project water in any year, it can be held in storage for future use  subject to 
storage capacity .  However, the City’s carryover is lost when Lake Berryessa spills.  Solano Project water is 
considered 99 percent reliable under normal water year conditions.  In the event of an extended drought, water 
would be made available by the Solano Irrigation District under the Solano Project Members’ Agreement as to 
Drought Measures and Water Allocation, dated as of March 1, 1999 (the “Solano Project Members’ Agreement”). 
State Water Project .   The North Bay Aqueduct ( the “North Bay Aqueduct”) is part of the State Water 
Project (the “State Water Project”).  The State Water Project exports water from northern  California to parts of the 
San Francisco Bay Area, San Joaquin Valley and southern California.  The SCWA has a contract with DWR for 
water supply from the State Water Project.  In turn, the SCWA has contracts with cities in the County of Solano, 
including the City, for provision of this water supply. 
The City has contract rights for up to 5,600 AF annually for State Water Project water delivered via the 
North Bay Aqueduct.  State Water Project water is taken from Lake Oroville through the Sacramento River via t he 
Barker Slough Pumping Plant and conveyed through the North Bay Aqueduct to the Cordelia Complex (a facility 
that contains the Cordelia Reservoir and the Cordelia Pump Station), where the City  then pumps the water to the 
Fleming Hill WTP.  The current State Water Project contract amount with the City could ultimately be reduced by 
1,125 AF  if the cities of Dixon and Rio Vista take their full North Bay Aqueduct contract amounts.   However, 
neither Dixon nor Rio Vista have the infrastructure to convey and treat such water.  
17 
If the yearly allocation is not used, a percentage can be carried over to the next year’s allocation but is 
subject to loss under certain circumstances.  There are also conveyance restrictions, such as limitations on pumping 
and exports from the Delta to protect fisheries habitats, and substantial reductions under drought conditions, with an  
estimated potential reduction of up to 100% in multiple dry water year conditions.  
Lakes Frey and Madigan .  The City holds pre -1914 Appropriative Right s to Lakes Frey and Madigan, 
located in northern Solano County.  The City owns both lakes and the surrounding land.  Water flows from Lake 
Madigan into Lake Frey and then into a diversion dam.  The water then flows through a pipe into the Green Valley 
WTP.  The water from these lakes is used solely by the Green Valley WTP and the available supply is equal to the 
maximum plant capacity.  Lakes Frey and Madigan currently have a storage capacity to meet a four -year water 
supply demand.   
Lake Curry.  Lake Curr y, located in Napa County, was formerly a source for both the City Water System 
and the Lakes Water System.  The City owns the lake and surrounding land.  Regulations enacted in 1992 resulted in 
the closure of the water treatment plant at Lake Curry and bl ocked further use of the lake as a water supply until an 
alternative means of conveyance could be found.  Since that time, the pipe which had once performed this function 
has been used to transport treated water under pressure from the Green Valley WTP to customers along the pipeline.  
The City has pursued a project to reestablish Lake Curry as a water source by studying alternative methods of 
conveyance and supporting ongoing scientific study and evaluation of local fish habitat. Due to uncertainties 
surrounding this project, the City is not including Lake Curry water in its 20-year water supply projections.

Water Agreements 
The City maintains water service agreements with three neighboring cities: American Canyon, Benicia and 
Fairfield.  Water transfer agreements typically invol ve one agency, the buyer, purchasing water for a specified 
period from another agency, the seller; the seller then reduces its available water supply by that amount.   A brief 
description of these agreements is provided below.  
City of American Canyon .  Sin ce 1996, the City has been under agreement with the City of American 
Canyon to sell American Canyon a permanent supply of potable water, to treat American Canyon excess raw water, 
and to provide facilities to transmit American Canyon water to certain areas  within the American Canyon water 
service area.  Under this agreement and five subsequent amendments, the City currently provides up to 2,074 AF per  
19 
year of potable water treatment capacity, with one remaining option for American Canyon to purchase an addi tional 
566 AF for a total of 2,640 AF of capacity. In addition, American Canyon is entitled to purchase up to 500 AF of 
raw water in an emergency, and under a tri -party wheeling agreement, has obtained 500 AF of the City’s Vallejo 
Permit Water and has permanently transferred the same amount to the City of Calistoga.  The agreement is in effect 
until mutually terminated by American Canyon and Vallejo .  In Fiscal Year s 2019-20 and 201 8-19, the City 
received $562,682 and $333,248, respectively, relating to water sales from the agreement with the City of American 
Canyon.  
City of Benicia.  Since 1962, the City has been under agreement with the City of Benicia to sell to Benicia 
either treated or untreated water; however, Benicia does not currently have the inter connection required for delivery 
of treated water.  Under this agreement and two subsequent amendments, Benicia may purchase up to 1,100 AF per 
year of the City’s Solano Project water.  The agreement is in effect until February 28, 2025.   Benicia may 
unilaterally terminate the agreement upon one year’s notice when it has provided facilities for an adequate new 
water supply.  Both Benicia and the City have assumed the agreement will be renewed at its expiration and have 
included the 1,100 AF in supply -demand projections.  In Fiscal Year s 2019-20 and 2018-19, the City received no 
revenue relating to water sales from the agreement with the City of Benicia.     
City of Fairfield.  The City has two agreements with the City of Fairfield.  The first agreement, date d July 
28, 2020 (the “Emergency Water Service Agreement”), requires Fairfield to supply treated water to the Lakes Water 
System service area, including areas commonly known as Suisun Valley, Gordon Valley, Rockville and portions of 
“old town” Cordelia, on an emergency basis.  Pursuant to the agreement’s terms, Fairfield must supply water up to a 
maximum flow rate of 2,000 gallons per minute, at a minimum pressure of 45 pounds per square inch, from two 
pump stations.  The City can supply the raw water and pa y for the cost of services while Fairfield is obligated to 
treat and deliver the water to the Lakes Water System.  In such event, the amount transferred would be deducted 
from Fairfield’s Solano Project water allocation and added to the City’s Solano Proje ct water allocation.  The 
agreement is in effect until terminated by either party with 90 days written notice.  During Fiscal Years 2019-20 and 
2018-19, Fairfield did not provide any water on an emergency basis to the City. 
The second agreement, dated June 3, 2014, requires Fairfield to supply treated water to  the Lakes Water 
System service area.  Pursuant to the agreement’s terms, Fairfield must supply water up to a maximum flow rate of 
21,000 gallons per hour for use by customers within the Lakes Water Sy stem.  A higher flow rate is allowed under 
the agreement but will trigger the payment of additional connection fees by Vallejo to Fairfield.  The agreement 
terminated Fairfield’s obligation to provide emergency potable water services to the area commonly k nown as “old 
town” Cordelia under the Emergency Water Service Agreement.  The agreement is in effect until terminated by the 
City.  The City may unilaterally terminate the agreement with 30 days’ written notice.  Fairfield, upon 10 days’ 
written notice to the City, may shut off water service for unpaid charges that are 90 days or more past due.  In Fiscal 
Years 2019-20 and 2018-19, the City paid $ 48,846 and $47,310, respectively, relating to water purchases from the 
agreement with the City of Fairfield. 
Future Supply for the Water Enterprise 
The City’s 2015 Urban Water Management Plan (the “2015 UWMP”) contains provisions for its water 
shortage contingency plan.  The City’s allocation of water is currently greater than its projected needs as 
demonstrated in the 2015 UWMP. The 2015 UWMP concludes that the City’s combined projected water supplies 
are anticipated to be sufficient to meet projected demands during normal, single -dry and multiple -dry water year 
conditions through 2040.  A new Urban Water Management Plant is expected to be adopted in 2021. 
Water Shortage Contingency Plan.  The City’s Water Shortage Contingency Plan (“WSCP”) addresses the 
short-term or emergency water management practices required during a drought or other water shortage condition.  
The WSCP helps the City consider impacts of short-term supply deficiency, including financial hardship on both the 
community and the City.  The City uses shortage planning to anticipate drought conditions  and prepare for impacts 
that may occur (e.g. supply shortages, economic impacts on the community and reduced revenue).  During the recent 
drought, the City adopted statewide conservation measures, but due to Vallejo’s adequate water supply, the City did 
not have to implement the WSCP or any restrictive stages of the WSCP.   
20 
On a regional level, the Solano Project Members’ Agreement requires specific drought measures by all 
participating agencies when the water level in Lake Berryessa drops below 800,000 AF as measured on December 1.

Capital Improvement Program 
On July 28, 2020, the City Council adopted the City’s five -year capital improvement program for Fiscal 
Years 2020 -2021 to 2024- 25 (the “Citywide CIP”), which, amo ng other things, addresses the current and future 
needs of the Water Enterprise.  The Water Enterprise’s portion of the Citywide CIP focused on compliance with the 
federal Safe Drinking Water Act (the “Safe Drinking Water Act”), SWRCB requirements and California Department 
of Safety of Dams (“DSOD”) requirements, replacing aging infrastructure and ensuring reliability and sufficient 
capacity of the City’s water pipelines.  In July 2019, the City Council received the City of Vallejo Water Rate Study, 
dated J une 26, 2019 (the “2019 Water Rate Study”), which focused on developing updated water rates and a 
financial plan for the City Water System that would allow the Water Enterprise to undertake replacement and repair 
of some of its maintenance priorities.  The  Water Enterprise’s capital needs for the City Water System are included 
in the 2019 Water Rate Study.  The following discussion of the City’s capital improvement program takes into 
consideration both the Citywide CIP and the 2019 Water Rate Study, collect ively referred to herein as the “CIP” or 
the “Capital Improvement Program”.   
The Citywide CIP projects are primarily grouped into seven categories: (i) water treatment plants,  
(ii) pipelines, (iii) pump stations, (iv) treated water storage facilities, (v ) water reservoirs and dams, (vi) meters and 
(vii) miscellaneous.  Below is a description of the primary purpose of projects within each category. 
Water Treatment Plants .   The three water treatment plants operated by the City have undergone major 
renovation or replacement in the last 25 years.  The Citywide CIP includes projects focused on upgrading treatment 
equipment and systems, replacing filter media, and improving chemical storage and injection systems to continue 
meeting Surface Water Treatment Rule Regulations.  The Citywide CIP includes approximately $7.6 million worth 
of water treatment plant improvements ( the City expected to fund approximately $4.6 million from the proceeds of 
the Series 2021A Bonds , and approximately $2.8 million from Water Ente rprise operat ing revenues and fund 
balances (“Pay-Go”)).  
Pipelines.  The City has 110 miles of pipeline over 70 years old.  The City plans to invest in its pipeline 
replacement program to reach a 100 -year replacement cycle. The Citywide CIP includes proje cts to rehabilitate, 
replace and install new distribution and transmission pipelines on an ongoing basis to improve system reliability, 
increase fire flows, reduce water loss due to leakage and improve water quality.  The City’s planned 5 -year water 
main replacement project will increase from the current one mile replaced annually, to over three  miles replaced 
annually.  The Citywide  CIP includes approximately $21.5 million worth of pipeline improvements ( the City 
expects to fund approximately $12.2 million from proceeds of the Series 2021A Bonds , and approximately $9.3M 
from Pay-Go).  
Pump Stations.  The Citywide CIP includes projects that will upgrade and retrofit pump stations to improve 
efficiency and reliability. The City actively works with Pacific Gas  & Electric to identify cost -effective capital 
projects which will reduce needed pumping power.  The City also plans to construct the Capital Zone 
Hydropneumatic Pump Station, purchase and install new motors and pumps, add new grid pumps at Fleming Hill 
Water Treatment Plant,  add emergency generators to critical pump stations and make modifications to existing 
pumps to reduce energy consumption.  The Capital Zone Hydropneumatic Pump Station project replaces an 
underground, treated water hydropneumatic pump station with an above -ground facility in a new location, with the 
goal of creating greater efficiency and accessibility with easier and safer maintenance.  The Cordelia generator 
project will provide a backup generator for the City’s largest supply pump s tation, which will provide greater 
reliability against power shut -offs.  The Citywide CIP includes approximately $6.3 million worth of pump station 
improvements (all of which are expected to be funded with proceeds of the Series 2021A Bonds). 
Treated Water Storage Facilities.  Funds are budgeted annually from operating revenues to perform various 
rehabilitation and repair work on existing reservoir tanks that includes painting, roof repair, vent replacement, and 
concrete work.  In addition, certain more sig nificant capital replacement projects are included as a part of Citywide 
CIP.  Projects include replacing the Rockville and Siebe tanks in the Lakes Water System, to provide additional 
operating and fire storage, providing upgrades to the City’s 6 million gallon Skyview Tank, and replacing the City’s 
Burnham Street gravity tank and hydropnuemetic tank which are both past their useful design life. The Citywide 
CIP includes approximately $4.2 million for treated water storage projects (all of which are expect ed to be funded  
with proceeds of the Series 2021A Bonds).   
22 
Dams.  The City Water System includes three large surface water reservoirs (Curry, Madigan, and Frey), 
all of which have dams.  The City also has two treated water reservoirs with dams (Fleming Hil l Clearwll and 
Swanzy Reservoir), and one raw water supply reservoir with a dam (Summit Reservoir). The DSOD works closely 
with the City by participating in annual inspections, working together through an annual maintenance program to 
address maintenance, infrastructure, safety, and continued water supply conveyance.  The DSOD then recommends 
certain improvements be completed in certain timeframes.  The Citywide CIP currently includes approximately $2.5 
million for dam improvements ( the City expects to fund  approximately $500,000 from the proceeds the Series 
2021A Bonds and $2 million from Pay-Go).  The City anticipates more funding may be needed in years to come. 
The majority of the City’s dams are deemed satisfactory by the DSOD, with Curry and Summit rank ed as 
fair. The age of the dam infrastructure ranges between  50 to 130 years old, and therefore the DSOD has requested 
improving the dams with new valves and higher capacity outlet structures, as well  as adding instrumentation.  The 
City is still estimating the total costs of these improvements, and working with the State to spread the costs of these 
improvements over an extended timeframe.  The City is also working to secure grants for these improvements, 
which could significantly reduce the improvement costs. 
Meter Replacement Projects .  The City budgets annually for the replacement of aged, broken and under -
registering meters, with an emphasis on large meters, for the purpose of accurate and equitable water volume 
measuring and billing.  The City currentl y plans to replace 80% of the city’s older inefficient meters, and to move 
toward an advanced metering infrastructure (AMI), to promote conservation and increase revenue.  The Citywide 
CIP includes approximately $14.1 million for meters , which is expected to be funded from proceeds of the Series 
2021A Bonds and Pay-Go. 
Miscellaneous Projects.  The Citywide CIP includes approximately $8.5 million for miscellaneous projects 
(the City expects to fund $5.5million from proceeds of the Series 2021A Bonds and $3.0 million Pay-Go).  Projects 
included in the category are upgrading the Water Department’s supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) 
system, providing security upgrades to water system facilities, and providing investments for updated management 
software. 
The engineering consulting firm, Kennedy Jenks, reviewed the City’s infrastructure condition and ability to 
meet water demand and regulatory requirements as part of the City’s 2015 Water Master Plan, which also provided a 
20-year Capital Improvement Plan.  Myriad Engineering, Inc. provided a long -term 30 -year CIP Development 
Program.  The findings of both studies have aided the City in setting future CIP priorities, but more recently 
discovered needs were considered as well.  The current cost estimate  of CIP projects for the Water Systems for 
Fiscal Years 2021 to 2025 is approximately $64.5 million ($69 million with inflation).  The budget for each project 
needs to be established and approved on an annual basis by the City Council, and adjustments to s uch budgets 
require approval of the City Counc il.  For Fiscal Year 2021, $4.9 million  was previously approved by Vallejo City 
Council (and the non- bond funded 5 -Yr CIP was presented).  Any additional funds added to F iscal Year 2020-21 
will require the Citywide CIP be amended and approved by City Council. 
The Series 2021A Bonds are intended to fund $51,272,855.76 of the total $64.5 million.  Over the next five 
Fiscal Years, the City expects to fund the remainder of the CIP projects by using Pay -Go.  The City will continue to 
pursue opportunities to secure grants for CIP, however, there is no guarantee that such grants will be secured.    
See “WATER ENTERPRISE FINANCES – Rate Setting and Budget Process” and “CONSTITUTIONAL 
LIMITATIONS ON TAXES AND WATER RATES AND CHARGES – Articles XIIIC and XIIID of the California 
Constitution.”
